body:
Somatic Experiencing for Third Eye Opening

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 am, staring at the ceiling, heart pounding, mind racing. You've been searching for answers, desperately trying to make sense of what's happening to you. Someone mentioned "third eye opening" or "kundalini awakening" and now you're down a rabbit hole, feeling more confused and scared than ever. I get it. I've been there. That raw, visceral terror that makes you question your sanity. The feeling that your body is betraying you, your mind is unraveling, and no one understands. You're not alone, even though it feels like you're the only one experiencing this hell.

I'm not here to tell you it's easy. It's not. I'm here to tell you it's survivable, and there are tools that can help you move through it, not just endure it. One of those tools, a really powerful one, is Somatic Experiencing (SE). You might've heard of it, or maybe it's completely new. Either way, stick with me. This isn't some fluffy New Age concept. This is about your nervous system, your body, and how they're reacting to something truly intense that's happening within you. You're not broken; you're just going through a deep, albeit messy, transformation. Right?

When people talk about "third eye opening," they often picture some blissful, enlightened state. They don't talk about the terror, the disorientation, the sensory overload, the feeling like you're losing your mind. They don't talk about the trauma that gets dredged up, the old wounds that suddenly feel fresh and raw. But here's the truth: that's often a huge part of it. That's the part that sends you searching for answers at 3 am. That's where SE comes in.

What Exactly Is Happening to You?

Look, whether you call it kundalini, spiritual emergency, or just a really bad breakdown, your body is reacting. Your nervous system is in overdrive. It's like your internal alarm system has been triggered and can't shut off. You might be experiencing:

  • Intense anxiety or panic attacks that come out of nowhere.
  • Uncontrollable shaking, tremors, or muscle spasms.
  • Extreme sensitivity to light, sound, or touch.
  • Disorientation, feeling detached from your body or reality.
  • Sudden, vivid memories or emotions from your past, often traumatic ones.
  • Insomnia, or wild, disturbing dreams.
  • Physical pain, pressure in your head, spine, or other parts of your body.
  • A sense of overwhelming energy moving through you.
  • Feeling utterly alone and misunderstood.

Does that sound familiar? I bet it does. I've felt every single one of those, and then some. It's terrifying because it feels like there's no escape. You're trapped in your own body, your own mind, and it's screaming. What's happening is often an activation of deeply held trauma, whether it's from childhood, past experiences, or even ancestral stuff. This "spiritual awakening" or "third eye opening" isn't just about pretty visions; it's about clearing out the old junk so something new can emerge. And clearing out old junk is messy, painful work. It stirs up everything you've repressed, everything your nervous system has been trying to protect you from. Know what I mean?

The Nervous System on Overload

Your nervous system has two main states: sympathetic (fight, flight, freeze) and parasympathetic (rest and digest). When you're going through something like this, your sympathetic nervous system is stuck in overdrive. It thinks you're in mortal danger, even when you're just lying in bed. This isn't a failure on your part; it's a biological response. Your body is trying to deal with an overwhelming amount of energy and information. It's trying to process things it couldn't process before, things that were shut down or frozen.

The "third eye opening" can feel like a direct assault on this system. It's like someone cranked the volume up on reality, and your nervous system just can't cope. It's not equipped to handle that much raw input without feeling threatened. So it goes into protection mode, which manifests as all those terrifying symptoms. You're not going crazy; your nervous system is just doing its job, albeit a bit too enthusiastically right now.

How Somatic Experiencing Helps

So, you're experiencing all this intense stuff, and your nervous system is freaking out. What do you do? This is where Somatic Experiencing comes in. SE, developed by Peter Levine, isn't about talking endlessly about your past traumas. It's about working with your body's inherent wisdom to release trapped energy and regulate your nervous system. It's about completing the biological responses that got stuck.

Think of it like this: when an animal in the wild is threatened, it fights, flees, or freezes. If it freezes, and then the threat passes, it'll often shake vigorously to discharge that trapped energy. Humans, especially in our "civilized" society, often don't allow ourselves to complete these natural responses. We freeze, and then we just stay frozen, holding all that survival energy in our bodies. This is what leads to chronic anxiety, panic, and all sorts of physical symptoms. When a kundalini awakening or third eye opening happens, it's like a huge jolt of energy trying to force that trapped energy to release, and if your system isn't ready for it, it can be incredibly overwhelming.

SE helps you do this safely, slowly, and in a controlled way. It's not about forcing anything. It's about titration - releasing small bits of energy at a time, so you don't get re-traumatized or overwhelmed. It's about pendulation - moving between states of activation and states of calm, so your nervous system learns it can come back down.

Here's what SE might look like in practice:

  • Tracking Sensations: Your practitioner will guide you to pay attention to physical sensations in your body. Not just emotions, but actual physical feelings - tingling, heat, pressure, cold, numbness. "Where do you feel that anxiety in your body? What's the quality of it?"
  • Titration: Instead of diving headfirst into the most intense sensation, you'll focus on the edges, or on a less intense part of the feeling. You'll allow just a small bit of that energy to surface, process it, and then move back to a place of relative calm.
  • Pendulation: You'll go back and forth between experiencing the difficult sensations and noticing resources that bring you a sense of safety or calm. This could be feeling your feet on the ground, noticing a pleasant object in the room, or even just feeling the chair beneath you. This teaches your nervous system that it can return to safety.
  • Discharge: As you process, you might notice involuntary movements - trembling, shaking, yawning, deep breaths, stomach gurgling. These are signs that your body is discharging trapped survival energy. It's a good thing, even if it feels strange.
  • Resourcing: You'll build internal and external resources. This means identifying things that make you feel safe, calm, and grounded. It could be a person, a place, an object, an activity, or even an imagined safe space.

The goal isn't to get rid of the sensations. It's to learn to tolerate them, to understand them, and to allow your body to complete its natural healing cycle. When you integrate these experiences, you're not just surviving; you're truly healing. You're making space for the true "third eye opening" - one that's integrated, grounded, and actually useful.

Integration, Not Eradication

You're not trying to shut down the "opening." That's often impossible and, frankly, not the point. The point is to integrate it, to make it part of you in a way that doesn't overwhelm you. Imagine trying to drink from a firehose. You'd drown. SE helps you adjust the pressure, so you can take sips, digest them, and slowly, over time, learn to drink from that powerful source without being knocked over.

This process of integration is slow. It's not a quick fix. You're rewiring your nervous system, and that takes time, patience, and often, a lot of courage. But here's the thing: you've already shown immense courage by even looking for answers. You're already on the path. You just need the right tools and guidance.

Why Somatic Work Is Essential for Spiritual Emergency

Honestly, a lot of spiritual traditions talk about these intense experiences but don't give you the tools to deal with the physiological fallout. They tell you to meditate more, or surrender, or just "be with it." And while those things have their place, when your nervous system is in full-blown panic, telling yourself to "surrender" can feel like telling a drowning person to just "relax." It's not helpful. It can even be re-traumatizing.

Spiritual emergency, kundalini crisis, third eye opening gone wild - whatever you call it - it's a body experience. It's happening in your cells, in your nerves, in your muscles. You can't just think your way out of it. You have to work with the body. That's why SE is so vital. It bridges the gap between the intense spiritual experiences and the very real physical and psychological impact they have on you.

I've seen so many people, myself included, try to intellectualize or meditate their way through this, only to find themselves more dysregulated. You're dealing with something primal. You need a primal approach. Somatic work gives you that. It honors the body's wisdom. It trusts that your body knows how to heal, if only given the right conditions and support.

You might be reading this and thinking, "But I don't have trauma!" Trust me, if you're experiencing this level of nervous system dysregulation, there's something there. It might not be "big T" trauma like war or abuse, but it could be "little t" traumas - chronic stress, neglect, medical procedures, difficult relationships, or even just growing up in a world that doesn't validate your sensitivity. Your body remembers everything, and these spiritual activations are often stirring up all that unprocessed material.

I'll tell you what, when I was in the thick of my own kundalini crisis, reading books like The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) or The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) helped me understand I wasn't alone. They provided a approach for what was happening. But it was the somatic work that actually helped me move through the terror in my body. It was the practical application of understanding the nervous system that made the difference. Knowing what was happening was one thing; actually doing something about it was another.

Finding Support and Practical Steps

So yeah, this is heavy stuff, and you shouldn't try to do it alone. Finding a qualified Somatic Experiencing practitioner is key. Look for someone who understands spiritual emergency or has experience with kundalini. Not all SE practitioners will have this specialized knowledge, so ask around. It's okay to interview a few people to find someone who feels right for you. Your safety and comfort are critical.

In the meantime, while you're looking for a practitioner, there are some simple things you can start doing to help ground yourself:

  • Orienting: Look around the room. Notice 5 things you can see, 4 things you can feel, 3 things you can hear, 2 things you can smell, and 1 thing you can taste. This brings your attention back to the present moment and helps to anchor you.
  • Grounding: Feel your feet on the floor. Really press them down. Notice the contact points. If you're sitting, feel your sit bones on the chair. Imagine roots growing from your feet into the earth.
  • Self-Touch: Place your hands on your body - on your chest, your belly, your arms. Feel the warmth of your hands. This can be incredibly soothing and help you feel more connected to your body.
  • Movement: Gentle movement can help discharge energy. Shake your hands, your feet. Stretch. Go for a slow walk in nature. Don't push yourself into intense exercise if your system is already overwhelmed.
  • Nature: Spend time outdoors. Even just sitting in a park, feeling the sun, listening to the birds. Nature has a naturally regulating effect on the nervous system.
  • Breath: Don't try to force deep breathing if it feels activating. Instead, focus on a gentle, slow exhale. Make your exhale slightly longer than your inhale. This activates the parasympathetic nervous system.
  • Community: Find others who understand. Online forums, support groups, or even just one trusted friend who won't dismiss your experience. Books like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) can also be a lifeline, making you feel less isolated.

You're not making this up. What you're experiencing is real. It's intense, it's scary, and it's a deep initiation. But you have the capacity to move through it. Your body has an innate ability to heal, to self-regulate, and to come back into balance. Somatic Experiencing provides the gentle, informed guidance to help your body remember how to do that. It’s not about fighting what’s happening; it’s about learning to dance with it, slowly, carefully, until the rhythm becomes yours, and the terror subsides, replaced by a deeper understanding of yourself and the world.

You've got this. Seriously. One breath, one sensation, one moment at a time. The dawn will come, and you'll be stronger for having walked through this night.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
